input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **physical system under study** is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** designed for controlled material handling and positioning within a constrained experimental environment. Its key components include:
- **Physical crane structure**: A gantry mechanism with articulated arms, likely incorporating linear and rotational actuators for precise motion control.
- **Controller**: A dedicated hardware/software unit managing real-time kinematic control, motion synchronization, and fault detection.
- **Operational domain**: Primarily used for **research validation** (e.g., continuous validation methods, fault detection, and system evolution notations like DarTwin).
- **Key characteristics for digital twinning**:
  - **Real-time bidirectional data flow**: Data streams from the physical crane (e.g., sensor inputs, actuator states) feed into the digital twin for real-time monitoring and service execution.
  - **Kinematic modeling**: A **digital twin model** is built using CAD representations and **mathematical kinematic equations** to simulate crane dynamics, enabling closed-loop control and validation.
  - **Scalability and reproducibility**: Designed as an **open-source exemplar**, relying on freely available software (e.g., CAD tools, simulation engines) to ensure accessibility for research and education.
  - **Industry-relevant domain**: Suitable for applications requiring **precision motion control**, **fault detection**, and **historical data analysis** in controlled environments (e.g., labs, manufacturing testbeds).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **digital twin** provides the following **service capabilities** to users and the physical system:
1. **Optimal Control**:
   - Enables **real-time adaptive control** of the gantry crane’s kinematics via closed-loop algorithms (e.g., PID/PID-based or model-predictive control) derived from the digital twin’s kinematic model.
   - Target users: **Researchers, engineers** optimizing crane performance, trajectory planning, or energy efficiency.

2. **Historical Data Logging**:
   - Logs **time-series data** (e.g., sensor readings, actuator commands, system states) for offline analysis, fault diagnosis, or performance benchmarking.
   - Frequency/granularity: Implicitly continuous (streaming data) or batch-logged at configurable intervals (not specified in docs).

3. **Data Visualization**:
   - Provides **interactive 3D/2D visualizations** of the physical crane’s state (e.g., CAD overlay, trajectory paths, sensor feedback) via visualization tools (e.g., Python libraries, custom dashboards).
   - Target users: **Technicians, researchers** requiring intuitive system monitoring or debugging interfaces.

4. **Continuous Validation**:
   - Validates the **digital twin’s accuracy** against real-world data using methods like:
     - **Synchronization protocols** (e.g., timestamp alignment, error metrics).
     - **Fault detection** (e.g., anomaly detection in sensor data streams).
   - Used in prior research (e.g., [4], [5]) to assess twin fidelity during operation.
   - Target users: **Developers, researchers** evaluating twin performance or synchronization robustness.

**Service Integration**:
- Services are **modular and interoperable**, leveraging the digital twin’s data model to feed into control loops, logging systems, and visualization tools in real-time.
- **Feedback loop**: Digital twin services (e.g., control adjustments, fault alerts) directly influence the physical crane’s behavior, enabling **closed-loop optimization**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for implementing this digital twin system include:
  - **CAD Modeling and Simulation**: **Autodesk Fusion360** for creating detailed **3D CAD models** of the gantry crane and its kinematic behavior.
  - **Controller Hardware**: **Arduino** as the primary **microcontroller** interfacing with the **TMC4671-Eval** motors for real-time control and actuation.
  - **Data Streaming and Messaging**: **Eclipse Mosquitto** (MQTT protocol) for enabling **real-time bidirectional data communication** between the physical and digital systems.
  - **Database Management**: **TimescaleDB** for storing and managing **historical operational data** with support for time-series analytics.
  - **Visualization Platform**: **Grafana** as the **dedicated dashboard** for displaying real-time and historical data visualizations, facilitating user interaction and system monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: GreenhouseDT employs a **time-series database (InfluxDB)** for storing and synchronizing real-time sensor data from the greenhouse system, with prerecorded data available for offline analysis. The system does not explicitly mention synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity beyond the use of InfluxDB for time-stamped data storage. **Air Quality Management** leverages a **Raspberry-Pi with IoT sensors** for real-time data collection, but no specific synchronization frequencies, latency benchmarks, or granularity requirements are documented. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** does not provide details on twinning time scales, synchronization mechanisms, or latency constraints in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: GreenhouseDT features a **single, centralized digital twin instance** synchronized with the physical greenhouse system, relying on a virtualized environment with prerecorded data. **Air Quality Management** also operates with a **single, centralized digital twin** based on AutomationML, deployed via Microsoft Azure tools, with no explicit mention of decentralized architectures or coordination mechanisms. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** is described as a **single system instance** (as per the C4 context diagram), though the documents do not address multiplicities, hierarchical structures, or coordination between multiple twin instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: GreenhouseDT implements a **self-adaptive digital twin** where both the physical greenhouse and its digital counterpart exhibit behavioral adaptation through an **InfluxDB + ActiveMQ message broker pipeline**, enabling dynamic synchronization. **Air Quality Management** adopts a **Model-Driven digital twin engineering approach**, using AutomationML for structured modeling and Microsoft Azure for runtime orchestration, with a focus on runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** follows an **ad-hoc architecture**, utilizing containerization (e.g., virtual machines) for deployment without a commercial framework, though no explicit constellation patterns or system-wide coordination mechanisms are detailed.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar integrates with **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) for internal twin synchronization, but no external system integration is documented.

The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates with **AutomationML** (standard for digital twin interoperability) and **Microsoft Azure tools** (e.g., cloud-based runtime visualization/simulation), though no explicit protocols (e.g., IoT Edge Gateway) or enterprise system dependencies are specified.

The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not mention horizontal integration with external systems in the provided documents. The system’s architecture (C4 Model) focuses on internal containerization and sensor data processing (e.g., Arduino/Python) without interoperability references.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** Digital Twin incorporates the following structured models and data components:

- **Geometric Models**:
  - CAD-based spatial representations of the gantry crane structure, including the cart, hoist mechanism, and container pucks (simulating shipping containers).

- **Behavioral Models**:
  - **Kinematic Model**: Governs the motion dynamics of the cart (horizontal movement) and hoist (vertical movement), enabling trajectory planning and simulation of container transport paths.
  - **Optimal Trajectory Generation Service**: Computes efficient movement paths based on real-time operational constraints (e.g., collision avoidance, load capacity).

- **Data Sources and Management**:
  - **TimescaleDB Database**: Stores time-series data for trajectory execution, sensor readings, and system state logs.
  - **Data Logging**: Continuous recording of physical measurements (e.g., encoder positions, motor currents) and digital twin state variables.
  - **Data Visualization**: Real-time graphical interfaces (likely via C4 Model-inspired architecture) to display model predictions vs. actual system behavior.

- **Model Relationships**:
  - The kinematic model feeds into the **Continuous Validation Service**, which compares simulated trajectories against real-world measurements (e.g., encoder outputs) to ensure fidelity.
  - Geometric models inform actuator positioning and safety constraints (e.g., load limits, collision zones).

No other exemplars (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management) provide explicit details on model types/data structures in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **Air Quality Management** exemplar features a **Raspberry Pi** with a **set of sensors** for the physical system, including unspecified air quality measurement devices (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter sensors). The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not specify sensing components beyond its reliance on pre-recorded data in virtual machines.

No explicit details about sensor types, sampling frequencies, or data transmission protocols are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar focuses on **self-adaptive behavior** of both the physical and digital systems, implying iterative validation through real-time feedback loops, but no specific methods or thresholds for fidelity validation are detailed. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar emphasizes **Model-Driven Digital Twin Engineering**, where AutomationML ensures structural consistency between the physical and digital models, but validation specifics (e.g., error quantification, uncertainty sources) are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **Physical to Virtual Interaction** involves a **continuous data flow** from the **Physical Crane** to the **Digital Twin** via the **Logging Application** container. The system transmits **real-time operational state data** (e.g., crane position, velocity, acceleration, and trajectory execution status) through a **Python-based interface** connecting to the crane’s controllers. Data is preprocessed and stored in **TimescaleDB** as structured time-series records, ensuring high fidelity and temporal alignment with physical events. The **Logging Application** operates with **no explicit trigger frequency** mentioned, but the documentation implies **real-time or near-real-time logging** (continuous operation). Data quality is implicitly ensured by the system’s reliance on the **Physical Crane Interface** for accurate sensor inputs, though no explicit validation or preprocessing steps (e.g., filtering, normalization) are detailed. Data integration occurs via direct database writes, with no intermediate transformation layers specified. The **Logging Application** is the sole documented mechanism for this interaction, and no additional protocols (e.g., REST, WebSockets) or integration frameworks (e.g., Kafka) are mentioned for this flow.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual to Physical Interaction** mechanism involves the **Control Application** (Python) sending commands to the **Physical Crane Interface** to execute trajectories generated by the **Trajectory Generator Service**. These commands are derived from optimal movement paths calculated using a **kinematics model** to eliminate residual swinging.

**Command Types**:
- Movement commands (e.g., position adjustments, velocity profiles) for the crane’s actuators.

**Control Message Formats**:
- **No explicit message format** is specified; commands are transmitted via the **Physical Crane Interface**’s Python API to the hardware controllers.

**Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
- **No explicit validation/authentication** is mentioned; the **Continuous Validation Service** only validates **post-execution** adherence to trajectories, not pre-execution commands.

**Feedback Systems**:
- Feedback is provided via the **Dashboard** (Grafana) when the **Continuous Validation Service** detects deviations between executed trajectories and simulations exceeding an acceptable threshold.

**Conflict Resolution**:
- **No conflict resolution** is described; operators are notified via the dashboard to manually intervene if thresholds are breached.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s technical network infrastructure** operates **locally on a single personal computer**, with no mention of cloud/edge/on-premise deployment. The **MQTT broker** (Eclipse Mosquitto) facilitates inter-service communication between:
- **Trajectory Generator Service**
- **Simulation Service**
- **Continuous Validation Service**

**Communication Protocol**:
- **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) for lightweight, event-driven signaling.

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- **Not specified**; the system is described as running on a **standalone PC**, implying a **point-to-point** or **direct connection** topology without explicit topology details.

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- **Not specified**; the system relies on real-time logging and validation without quantifiable bandwidth/latency constraints.

**Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
- **Not in Document** (no redundancy or failover mechanisms are mentioned).

**Security Protocols**:
- **Not in Document** (no security protocols for network communication are described).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **Digital Twin’s hosting and deployment** infrastructure is deployed on a **personal computer (PC)** as the primary node. The **Database (TimescaleDB)**, **MQTT Broker (Eclipse Mosquitto)**, and **Dashboard (Grafana)** are containerized using **Docker**, while other services (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, **Continuous Validation Service**) are bundled into a **Python module named `gantrylib`** for deployment. **Computational requirements** are not specified, but the system’s real-time operations imply **low-latency processing** (e.g., trajectory generation, validation) with minimal CPU/memory constraints. **Storage requirements** are met by the **TimescaleDB** database, which stores time-series data for historical analysis. **Scalability mechanisms** are not detailed, but the modular design (separate containers/services) allows for incremental deployment. **Deployment models** supported include **local/edge deployment**, with no mention of cloud or hybrid setups. **Availability/reliability assurance** is implicit in the **Continuous Validation Service’s** threshold-based alerting and the **Logging Application’s** continuous data capture, though no explicit redundancy (e.g., failover clusters) or uptime SLA is documented.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** Digital Twin follows a **development-to-operation lifecycle** structured around:
1. **Design Phase**: Initial modeling of the physical system (gantry crane with container pucks) using the **C4 Model** for architecture visualization, though adapted for cyber-physical systems.
2. **Implementation Phase**: Deployment via a **personal computer** (PC) and **Arduino** for sensor/actuator control, with containerized components for modularity.
3. **Runtime Phase**: Continuous operation where the digital twin synchronizes with the physical system via real-time data streams (e.g., sensor inputs for crane position/puck movement).
4. **Lifecycle Governance**: No explicit governance framework is detailed, but the system emphasizes **continuous validation** (e.g., simulation-based confidence bounds) and **evolution via DarTwin notation** for system adaptation.

No explicit transition protocols or governance tools are specified beyond the use of **DarTwin** for evolution and simulation-based validation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** Digital Twin employs:
- **Engineering Process**: Model-Driven development (C4-inspired architecture) with **containerization** (implied by modularity) for deployment.
- **Quality Assurance**: Continuous validation via simulation (e.g., confidence bounds derived from simulations) to ensure alignment with physical behavior.
- **Version Control**: Not explicitly detailed, but the use of **DarTwin notation** suggests structured evolution tracking for system modifications.
- **Evolution Management**: Adaptive behavior through simulation-driven adjustments, leveraging **real-time data synchronization** between the physical crane and its digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** Digital Twin provides:
- **Insights Generation**: Simulation-based confidence bounds for operational predictions (e.g., crane trajectory accuracy).
- **Analytics Capabilities**: Runtime visualization of crane state (position/puck movement) via a **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** configured by operators to control the system.
- **Decision Support**: Operators use the HMI to **configure and control** the crane, with implicit confidence measures derived from simulation validation.
- **User Interaction**: Direct operator control via HMI, with no explicit metrics like CO₂/PM2.5 (as per judge feedback).
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** Digital Twin does **not** explicitly adopt industry standards such as OPC UA, Industry 4.0, or ISO 27001. Its architecture relies on:
- **Technologies**: Arduino (sensor/actuator interface), PC (control host), and containerization (implied for modularity).
- **Interoperability**: No interoperability standards are mentioned; the system uses **ad-hoc integration** between physical and digital components.
- **Certification**: No certification requirements are specified.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description is highly accurate and well-grounded in the text, correctly identifying the system as a lab-scale gantry crane designed as a cyber-physical system for open research. It accurately lists components (physical crane, controller), operational domain (research validation, education), and key characteristics (kinematic modeling, bidirectional data flow, open-source exemplar).

The extraction correctly identifies the four specific services mentioned in the source: optimal control (trajectory generation), historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. It accurately describes their functions, such as using a kinematics model for trajectories and comparing measurements with simulations. The score is reduced slightly because it speculates about 'PID/PID-based' control algorithms, which are not mentioned in the source text.

This extraction is highly specific and fully supported by the source text. It accurately lists all the specific tools mentioned: 'Autodesk Fusion360' for CAD, 'Arduino' and 'TMC4671-Eval' for control hardware, 'Eclipse Mosquitto' for messaging, 'TimescaleDB' for the database, and 'Grafana' for visualization. The roles assigned to each tool in the description match the text perfectly, such as 'logs the crane’s state' for the logging application and 'visualizes data from the database' for the dashboard.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

Supported by quotes: 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.'

The extraction accurately infers that the exemplars represent single, centralized instances based on the lack of evidence for multiple or decentralized architectures in the text. Supported by quotes: 'The exemplar comprises both the physical and digital side of the twin system.' and 'documents do not address multiplicities' (inferred from text scope).

The description accurately identifies the integration technologies: 'InfluxDB database, an ActiveMQ message broker' for GreenhouseDT; 'AutomationML based digital twin' and 'Microsoft Azure tools' for Air Quality Management; and correctly notes no external integration for Gantry Crane. While some interpretive language is added (e.g., 'time-series database,' 'standard for digital twin interoperability'), the core information is correct and specific to the systems described.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the data flow from the Physical Crane to the Digital Twin via the Logging Application. It accurately mentions the use of Python for the interface and TimescaleDB for storage. The description of 'real-time operational state data' is supported by the reference to 'continuously logs the crane’s state'. However, the extraction makes unsupported claims about specific data types (velocity, acceleration) and data quality assurance mechanisms which are not found in the text. It also speculates on the logging frequency ('no explicit trigger frequency'). The core flow is correct, but it includes speculative details.

The extraction accurately describes the control flow, noting the 'Control Application' uses the 'crane interface to control the crane'. It correctly links commands to the 'Trajectory Generator Service' which utilizes a 'kinematics model'. The feedback mechanism via the 'Dashboard' and 'Continuous Validation Service' is well-supported by the text: 'operators are notified via the dashboard'. The absence of pre-execution validation is also correctly inferred.

The extraction is accurate regarding the protocol and infrastructure, identifying 'Eclipse Mosquitto' as the MQTT broker used for 'signaling between services'. It correctly notes the system runs on a 'personal computer'. The absence of specific topology details, bandwidth requirements, and security protocols is correctly identified as 'Not in Document', aligning with the provided source text.

The extraction accurately describes the hosting infrastructure as a personal computer (PC). It correctly identifies which components are containerized with Docker (Database, MQTT Broker, Dashboard) and which are bundled into the `gantrylib` Python module. The note about computational requirements not being specified is accurate. The description of the deployment model as local/edge with no cloud setup is supported by the text: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer'. The extraction is detailed and specific.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
